title: Tame Your Upset Stomach with These 10 Herbal Tea Options
intro_desc: Herbal teas for upset stomach are a type of remedy that helps alleviate digestive discomfort by soothing the stomach and intestines. They contain natural ingredients that have anti-inflammatory and calming properties, which can ease nausea, bloating, and cramps associated with upset stomach. The best herbal teas for this issue include Zingiber officinale, Foeniculum vulgare, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Matricaria chamomilla, Taraxacum officinale, Mentha x piperita, Peumus boldus, Aloe barbadensis, Ginkgo biloba, and Cinchona officinalis. If left untreated, an upset stomach can lead to missed work or school, social isolation, and decreased productivity, negatively impacting one's overall quality of life and daily activities.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale tea, also known as ginger tea, is good for upset stomach because it helps to calm down the digestive system. When we eat something that doesn't agree with us, our stomach can get upset, causing nausea, vomiting, and stomach pain. Ginger tea works by reducing inflammation in the stomach and intestines, which helps to ease the discomfort. It also helps to move food through the digestive system faster, which can prevent the stomach from getting too full and causing pain. Additionally, ginger has natural anti-inflammatory properties that can help to soothe the stomach lining, reducing irritation and discomfort. As a result, ginger tea can provide quick relief from stomach upset, making it a popular remedy for nausea and digestive issues.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerol is not actually present (also known as English Lavender), but in Zingiber officinale (Ginger). However, the main bioactive compound that may help with an upset stomach is Linalool, which can help to soothe and calm the digestive system, reducing nausea and discomfort.

Shogaol is not found. However, I can provide information on Shogaol in general and Lavandula angustifolia (Lavender oil).  Shogaol is a compound found in Ginger, not Lavandula angustifolia. Here is a detailed sentence explaining why Ginger and Shogaol are good for upset stomach: Shogaol in Ginger helps to soothe an upset stomach by reducing inflammation, easing nausea, and calming the digestive tract, which can help to alleviate symptoms such as cramps, bloating, and discomfort.
Zingiberene helps with upset stomach because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce swelling and ease digestion problems.

remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal teas for upset stomach
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Lavandula angustifolia herbal tea that helps with an upset stomach, start by gathering dried lavender flowers. You can buy these from a health food store or online. Next, boil fresh water in a kettle or on the stove, then let it cool for a minute or two. Now, add one teaspoon of dried lavender flowers to a teapot or a cup. Pour the cooled water over the lavender flowers, making sure they are completely covered. Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes, depending on your preference for the tea's strength. After the steeping time, strain the tea into a cup using a strainer or a piece of cheesecloth to catch the lavender flowers. You can add a sweetener like honey if you prefer, but be careful not to add too much as the lavender flavor is delicate. Once you have your tea, you can enjoy it hot or cold, depending on your comfort. It's recommended to drink this tea after meals to help soothe your stomach and alleviate any discomfort.

remedies.remedy_desc: Foeniculum vulgare, also known as fennel, is a natural remedy for upset stomach. The tea made from its seeds has been used for centuries to soothe digestive issues. This is because fennel seeds contain compounds that help to relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, reducing cramps and spasms. Fennel tea also has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce swelling and inflammation in the digestive tract. Additionally, it has a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help to alleviate nausea and vomiting. The anise-like flavor of fennel tea can also help to stimulate digestion and relieve bloating. When consumed as a tea, fennel seeds can help to ease symptoms of indigestion, gas, and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Polyphenols are good for upset stomach because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to soothe and calm the digestive system, reducing irritation and discomfort.
Flavonoids are good for upset stomach because they help to ease inflammation and relax the muscles in the stomach, which can help to reduce nausea and discomfort.

Foeniculin is good for an upset stomach because it helps to soothe and calm the digestive system, reducing inflammation and easing nausea, cramps, and other discomforts associated with an irritated stomach.
Tannins are good for an upset stomach because they have a soothing effect on the digestive system, reducing inflammation and helping to calm nausea and cramps.
Anethol is good for upset stomach because it helps to soothe the digestive system and ease nausea by reducing inflammation and relaxing the muscles in the stomach.

remedies.image_alt: Foeniculum vulgare herbal teas for upset stomach
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Lavandula angustifolia herbal tea that helps with an upset stomach, start by gathering fresh or dried lavender flowers. If using fresh flowers, pick them in the morning after the dew has dried, and dry them immediately by tying them in small bunches and hanging them upside down in a warm, dry place. Once your lavender flowers are dry, you can use them to make your tea. To do this, add 1 teaspoon of dried lavender flowers to a cup of boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. Strain the tea into another cup and discard the lavender flowers. You can add honey to sweeten your tea if you like, but be sure to drink it while it's still warm. Lavender tea has natural calming properties that can help soothe an upset stomach, reduce nausea, and promote relaxation. To get the best results, drink your lavender tea in moderation, ideally 1-2 cups a day, and consult with a healthcare professional if your stomach symptoms persist or worsen.

remedies.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita tea, also known as peppermint tea, can help ease an upset stomach. The menthol in peppermint tea helps relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, reducing cramps and spasms that can cause discomfort. When you drink peppermint tea, the menthol also helps to slow down digestion and reduce the symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), such as bloating and gas. Additionally, peppermint tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the stomach lining and reduce inflammation that can cause stomach pain and nausea. This makes peppermint tea a popular remedy for stomach issues, including nausea, indigestion, and stomach cramps. Drinking peppermint tea can also help to calm the digestive system, making it easier to digest food and reducing the risk of stomach upset after eating.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Lavandula angustifolia herbal tea that helps with an upset stomach, start by gathering dried lavender flowers. You can buy these at a health food store or online. Begin by boiling 1 cup of water in a kettle or pot, then reduce the heat to a simmer. Next, add 1 to 2 teaspoons of dried lavender flowers to the water and let it steep for 5 to 7 minutes. As it steeps, the lavender will release its calming properties into the water. Once the tea is ready, strain it into a cup using a tea strainer or cheesecloth to remove the lavender flowers. Finally, you can add honey or lemon to taste, but be careful not to add too much as the lavender flavor can be strong. It's essential to note that pregnant women and people with allergies should consult with a doctor before consuming lavender tea, and it's also recommended to consult with a doctor if you have any underlying health conditions.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra tea, also known as licorice root tea, is often used to soothe an upset stomach. This is because it contains a compound called glycyrrhizin, which has anti-inflammatory properties. When we eat something bad or get a stomach bug, it can cause inflammation in the stomach lining, leading to pain and discomfort. Glycyrrhizin helps to reduce this inflammation, making it easier for the stomach to heal. Additionally, licorice root tea has a calming effect on the digestive system, helping to slow down digestion and give the stomach a break. This can help to prevent further irritation and allow the stomach to recover.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a soothing Lavandula angustifolia herbal tea for an upset stomach, you'll need fresh or dried lavender flowers. Start by boiling one liter of water in a kettle or pot, then reduce the heat to a simmer. Add 1-2 tablespoons of dried lavender flowers or 2-3 sprigs of fresh lavender to the water. Let the mixture steep for 5-10 minutes, allowing the lavender's calming properties to infuse into the tea. You can also add a small amount of honey or lemon to taste, but be sure to use a small amount as lavender can be quite potent. After the steeping time has passed, carefully strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or a tea strainer to remove the lavender flowers. Your lavender tea is now ready to drink, and it can help to calm an upset stomach and promote relaxation.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cinchona officinalis tea is a good remedy for an upset stomach. The main reason is that it contains quinine, a natural substance that helps calm down the stomach muscles. When you have an upset stomach, these muscles can get too active, causing nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Quinine in the tea helps to slow down these muscles, so they don't work too hard. This makes it easier to digest food and reduces the discomfort in the stomach. The tea also has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and irritation in the digestive system. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis tea has been used for centuries to treat fever and other stomach problems, making it a trusted natural remedy for many people. Its soothing properties can help ease stomach cramps and spasms, providing relief from the pain and discomfort of an upset stomach. The tea's natural antiseptic properties also help to prevent infections, which can further irritate the stomach and make symptoms worse.

After the steeping time is over, strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup to remove the lavender flowers. Discard the flowers and drink the tea while it's still warm. remedies.remedy_desc: Urtica dioica tea, also known as nettle tea, is a good remedy for upset stomach. remedies.remedy_desc: Taraxacum officinale tea, also known as dandelion tea, is a popular remedy for an upset stomach. The roots and leaves of the dandelion plant have been used for centuries to treat digestive issues. The tea is made by steeping the roots and leaves in hot water, releasing their healing properties. It's said to work by soothing the digestive system, reducing inflammation, and improving digestion. The tea is also a natural diuretic, which means it helps the body get rid of excess fluids and salts that can cause bloating and discomfort. Additionally, dandelion tea is rich in vitamins and minerals, such as potassium, which can help replace lost electrolytes and balance the body's fluids. When consumed, the tea is believed to ease symptoms of indigestion, nausea, and stomach cramps, providing relief from an upset stomach.

Your tea is now ready to drink. To enhance the calming effects and aid digestion, you can add a squeeze of fresh lemon juice and a drizzle of honey to taste. Be sure to drink the tea in moderation, as excessive consumption of lavender tea may cause drowsiness.

This can help alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach, such as bloating, gas, and stomach pain.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Lavandula angustifolia herbal tea that can help with an upset stomach, you will need a few simple ingredients and some basic equipment. First, gather a handful of dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers, also known as English lavender, and place them in a clean muslin bag or a tea infuser. Next, boil a pot of fresh water and then reduce the heat to a gentle simmer. Remove the pot from the heat and add the muslin bag or tea infuser to the water. Allow the flowers to steep for 5-7 minutes, depending on your desired strength of tea. As the flowers steep, the essential oils and flavonoids will be released into the water, creating a soothing and calming tea. Once the steeping time is up, carefully remove the muslin bag or tea infuser from the pot and pour the tea into a cup. You can add honey or lemon to taste, but be sure to drink the tea slowly and at room temperature, as hot liquids can exacerbate an upset stomach.

You can purchase these from a health food store or grow your own lavender plant in your garden. Next, place one teaspoon of the dried flowers into a tea infuser or a small muslin bag. If you don't have a tea infuser, you can simply add the flowers directly to a teapot or cup. Pour boiling water over the lavender flowers and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. Drink the tea slowly and mindfully, allowing the soothing properties of the lavender to calm your stomach and ease any discomfort.

supplementary_best: A combination of Zingiber officinale (ginger), Foeniculum vulgare (fennel), and Glycyrrhiza glabra (licorice root) is a good remedy for an upset stomach. Ginger has natural anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe the stomach and reduce nausea. Fennel seeds contain compounds that ease digestion and relax the muscles in the stomach, helping to calm stomach cramps and spasms. Licorice root has anti-inflammatory properties that protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation, which can cause stomach pain. This combination works well together because it addresses multiple causes of an upset stomach: ginger reduces nausea and inflammation, fennel eases digestion and relaxes stomach muscles, and licorice root protects the stomach lining and reduces inflammation. Drinking a tea made from equal parts of these herbs may provide relief from an upset stomach.
supplementary_avoid: If you have an upset stomach, it's best to avoid herbal teas that can irritate your digestive system. One such tea is the 'Rauwolfia serpentina' or Snakeroot tea. This tea contains a compound called reserpine, which can slow down digestion and worsen stomach cramps. Another tea to avoid is 'Ephedra sinica' or Ma Huang tea. Ephedra can cause stomach upset, nausea, and diarrhea due to its stimulating properties. 'Yohimbe bark' tea, made from the bark of 'Pausinystalia johimbe', is also not recommended. Yohimbe can stimulate the digestive system, but it can also cause stomach cramps, nausea, and diarrhea in some people. Similarly, 'Cola nitida' or Kola nut tea should be avoided as it can cause stomach upset and diarrhea due to its caffeine content. Finally, 'Cassia auriculata' or Sickle pod tea is not recommended as it can cause stomach irritation and worsen digestive issues in some individuals.
supplementary_other_remedies: Ginger, found in many Asian dishes, can help soothe an upset stomach. The active compound, gingerol, helps reduce inflammation and relax the muscles in the digestive tract. This can ease nausea and cramps. To use it, you can try ginger in its raw form, like fresh ginger roots or ginger juice, or take it in the form of ginger candies or supplements. Some people also use ginger as a spice in their cooking or drink ginger ale, which has a similar effect.  Aloe vera juice is another natural remedy that can help calm an upset stomach. The gel from the aloe vera plant contains compounds that can soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation. Drinking aloe vera juice after meals can help ease digestive discomfort and reduce symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). However, it's essential to choose a reputable brand and follow the recommended dosage, as excessive consumption can cause diarrhea or other side effects.  Probiotics are live bacteria and yeasts that are beneficial for gut health. They can help restore the balance of good bacteria in the digestive system, which can become disrupted due to various factors, such as antibiotics or a poor diet. Probiotics can help alleviate symptoms of IBS, reduce inflammation, and improve digestion. You can find probiotics in fermented foods like yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut, and kimchi, or take them as supplements.  Activated charcoal can also help alleviate digestive discomfort. It works by absorbing toxins and gas in the digestive system, which can help reduce bloating and discomfort. However, it's essential to note that activated charcoal can interfere with medication absorption, so it's best to take it separately from other medications. Additionally, some people may experience side effects like constipation or gas when taking activated charcoal.  Fennel seeds have been used for centuries to help alleviate digestive issues, including gas, bloating, and indigestion. The compounds in fennel seeds, such as anethole, can help relax the muscles in the digestive tract and reduce inflammation. To use fennel seeds, you can try chewing on them after meals or drinking fennel tea.  Slippery elm is a natural fiber that can help soothe an upset stomach. The mucilage in slippery elm can help coat the digestive tract and reduce inflammation. This can help alleviate symptoms of IBS, such as diarrhea or constipation, and reduce discomfort. You can find slippery elm in supplement form or drink slippery elm tea.  Bentonite clay can also help alleviate digestive issues. It works by absorbing toxins and gas in the digestive system, which can help reduce bloating and discomfort. However, it's essential to note that bentonite clay can interact with certain medications, so it's best to consult with a healthcare professional before using it.  These natural remedies can help alleviate digestive discomfort and promote overall gut health. However, it's essential to note that everyone's body is different, and what works for one person may not work for another.
supplementary_related_ailments: People who have an upset stomach often experience other problems like bloating, gas, diarrhea, and constipation. These issues can be caused by inflammation in the digestive tract, which can be reduced with the help of medicinal herbs. Peppermint, for example, is known to ease bloating and gas by relaxing the muscles in the digestive tract and improving digestion. Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation and alleviate nausea and vomiting. Licorice root can soothe the stomach lining and reduce inflammation, making it easier to digest food. Turmeric contains a compound called curcumin, which has potent anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of IBS (irritable bowel syndrome). Aloe vera juice can also help soothe the stomach lining and reduce inflammation, making it easier to digest food. These herbs can be consumed as teas, capsules, or added to food to provide relief from digestive problems.
